Why Do We Need Compilers, Interpreters, JIT Compilers, and Transpilers?
‚ÄúBefore learning programming languages, understand why they exist.‚Äù

Imagine This‚Ä¶
You travel to Japan.
You only speak English.
The shopkeeper only speaks Japanese.
How will you communicate?
You‚Äôll need someone who understands both languages.
That person translates your words into Japanese and the shopkeeper‚Äôs words back into English.
Programming languages work the same way.
Humans write code in languages like JavaScript, Java, Python, or C++ because they are easy to understand.
Computers, however, understand only one language:
Machine Code (0s and 1s)Somehow, our human-readable code must be converted into machine code.
This need gave birth to four important technologies:

‚Ä¢ Interpreter

‚Ä¢ Compiler

‚Ä¢ JIT Compiler

‚Ä¢ Transpiler
Let‚Äôs understand why each one exists.

The Fundamental Problem
When you write:
console.log("Hello World");the CPU doesn‚Äôt understand JavaScript.
It understands something more like this:
10110011
01101001
11010100So who converts your code?
That depends on the programming language.

The Interpreter
Imagine a Live Translator
Suppose you‚Äôre giving a speech.
A translator stands beside you.
You say one sentence.
The translator immediately translates it.
Then you speak another sentence.
Again, it gets translated.
Sentence by sentence.
That‚Äôs exactly how an interpreter works.
Your Code
‚Üì
Interpreter
‚Üì
Machine Code
‚Üì
CPUExample:
print("Hello")print("World")The interpreter reads:
Read Line 1
‚ÜìConvert‚ÜìExecute‚ÜìRead Line 2‚ÜìConvert‚ÜìExecuteIt never converts the entire program at once.

Advantages

‚Ä¢ Easier to debug

‚Ä¢ Faster development

‚Ä¢ Platform independent

‚Ä¢ Immediate execution

Disadvantages
Every time you run the program‚Ä¶
The interpreter translates it again.
And again.
And again.
That‚Äôs why interpreted languages are generally slower.

The Compiler
Now imagine something different.
Instead of carrying a translator everywhere‚Ä¶
You hire someone once.
He translates your entire English book into Japanese.
Now you can hand that translated book to anyone.
No translator is needed anymore.
This is exactly what a compiler does.
Entire Program‚ÜìCompiler‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìExecutable FileExample:
#include<iostream>int main(){
std::cout<<"Hello";
}The compiler generates something like
hello.exeWhen you double-click it,
the CPU runs it directly.
No translation is needed anymore.

Why Is C++ So Fast?
Because the translation has already been completed.
Execution becomes:
Executable‚ÜìCPUCan macOS run it?
No.
Can Linux run it?
No.
Because every operating system understands different machine instructions.
Sun Microsystems asked an important question:
Can we build one program that works everywhere?
Their solution changed software forever.

Bytecode and the JVM
Instead of generating machine code‚Ä¶
Java generates Bytecode.
Java Source Code‚ÜìJava Compiler‚ÜìBytecode‚ÜìJVM‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìCPUEvery operating system has its own JVM.
The JVM converts Bytecode into the machine language of that operating system.
That‚Äôs why Java became famous for:
Write Once, Run Anywhere

JavaScript Faced a Different Problem
JavaScript evolves every year.
Developers love writing modern syntax like this:
const add = (a, b) => a + b;But older browsers don‚Äôt understand arrow functions.
Should developers stop using modern JavaScript?
No.
Instead, someone invented a translator for JavaScript itself.

The Birth of Babel
Babel doesn‚Äôt convert JavaScript into machine code.
Instead‚Ä¶
It converts new JavaScript into older JavaScript.
Modern JavaScript‚ÜìBabel‚ÜìOlder JavaScriptExample:
Modern JavaScript
const square = x => x * x;After Babel
var square = function(x){
return x * x;
};Now even older browsers understand the code.
This process is called Transpiling.

So What Exactly Is a Transpiler?
A compiler converts:
High-Level Language‚ÜìMachine CodeA transpiler converts:
High-Level Language‚ÜìAnother High-Level LanguageExamples:

‚Ä¢ ES6 ‚Üí ES5

‚Ä¢ TypeScript ‚Üí JavaScript

‚Ä¢ JSX ‚Üí JavaScript
Notice something.
The output is still readable code.
Not machine code.

But Who Actually Runs JavaScript?
Many beginners think:
JavaScript‚ÜìCPUThat‚Äôs incorrect.
The real process is:
JavaScript‚ÜìJavaScript Engine‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìCPUEvery browser contains a JavaScript Engine.
Examples:

‚Ä¢ Google Chrome ‚Üí V8

‚Ä¢ Firefox ‚Üí SpiderMonkey

‚Ä¢ Safari ‚Üí JavaScriptCore
These engines understand JavaScript.

JavaScript Became Slow‚Ä¶
Initially,
JavaScript engines only interpreted code.
That meant every execution required translation.
Google engineers asked:
Can we make JavaScript faster?
The answer was the JIT Compiler.

JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler
Instead of choosing only compilation or only interpretation‚Ä¶
Modern JavaScript engines combine both.
JavaScript‚ÜìInterpreter‚ÜìFrequently Executed?‚ÜìYes‚ÜìJIT Compiler‚ÜìOptimized Machine CodeFrequently used code gets compiled into machine code.
Rarely used code remains interpreted.
This gives JavaScript both:

‚Ä¢ Fast startup

‚Ä¢ Fast execution
That‚Äôs why modern JavaScript is much faster than it was years ago.

Everything Together
C++
C++ Code‚ÜìCompiler‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìCPUPython
Python Code‚ÜìInterpreter‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìCPUJava
Java Code‚ÜìCompiler‚ÜìBytecode‚ÜìJVM‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìCPUModern JavaScript
Modern JavaScript‚ÜìBabel‚ÜìOlder JavaScript‚ÜìJavaScript Engine‚ÜìInterpreter + JIT Compiler‚ÜìMachine Code‚ÜìCPU‚úÖ Computers understand only machine code.
‚úÖ An Interpreter translates code line by line during execution.
‚úÖ A Compiler translates the entire program before execution.
‚úÖ Java uses Bytecode so the same program can run on multiple operating systems.
‚úÖ Babel is a transpiler that converts modern JavaScript into older JavaScript.
‚úÖ Modern JavaScript engines combine interpretation and JIT compilation for better performance.

Test Your Understanding
Try answering these questions without searching online:

‚Ä¢ Why can‚Äôt a CPU execute JavaScript source code directly?

‚Ä¢ Why is C++ generally faster than Python?

‚Ä¢ Why does Java compile to Bytecode instead of machine code?

‚Ä¢ What problem does Babel solve?

‚Ä¢ What‚Äôs the difference between a compiler and a transpiler?

‚Ä¢ Why was the JIT compiler invented?

‚Ä¢ Does Babel generate machine code? Why or why not?

‚Ä¢ Name one real-world scenario where a transpiler is useful besides Babel.
